answer.rationale: Pan-canonical lexical demonstration. John 1:1 (καὶ θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος kai theos ēn ho logos, 'and the Word was God', theos G2316 as predicate of ho logos G3056) opens the gospel by identifying the pre-incarnate Word as God. John 20:28 (ὁ κύριός μου καὶ ὁ θεός μου ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou, 'my Lord and my God') has Thomas address the risen Jesus with both articular nominatives functioning as vocative; Jesus does not correct him but commends his faith in v.29. Col 2:9 (πᾶν τὸ πλήρωμα τῆς θεότητος σωματικῶς pan to plērōma tēs theotētos sōmatikōs, 'the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily', theotēs G2320 = the abstract noun for the divine nature itself, distinct from theiotēs G2305) places the entire divine essence in the incarnate Christ. Heb 1:8 (πρὸς δὲ τὸν υἱόν Ὁ θρόνος σου ὁ θεός pros de ton huion ho thronos sou ho theos, 'but of the Son he says, Your throne, O God') quotes Ps 45:6 LXX and applies the address 'O God' directly to the Son. Titus 2:13 and 2 Pet 1:1 share the construction τοῦ μεγάλου θεοῦ καὶ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (a single article governing two nouns joined by kai, identifying Jesus Christ as 'our great God and Savior'). Phil 2:6 (ἐν μορφῇ θεοῦ ὑπάρχων en morphē theou hyparchōn, 'existing in the form of God', and τὸ εἶναι ἴσα θεῷ to einai isa theō, 'being equal with God') places Christ in the divine form before the incarnation. John 8:58 (πρὶν Ἀβραὰμ γενέσθαι ἐγὼ εἰμί prin Abraam genesthai egō eimi, 'before Abraham was, I am') applies the Exod 3:14 self-naming to Jesus.
answer.notes: Full deity of Christ is the classical Trinitarian boundary, fixed at Nicaea AD 325 against Arius. The verdict rests on pan-canonical lexical demonstration. John identifies the Word as theos at 1:1, monogenēs theos G3439 plus G2316 at 1:18, with Thomas's confession at 20:28 forming the bookend. Paul applies divine attributes and the title theos to Christ at Col 1:15-20, Col 2:9, Phil 2:6-11, Rom 9:5, Titus 2:13. Hebrews 1 has the Father address the Son as ho theos at 1:8. Peter joins the chorus at 2 Pet 1:1. The Granville-Sharp construction at Titus 2:13 and 2 Pet 1:1 (one article governing two nouns joined by kai, both referring to the same person) is grammatically tight. Cult-marker-if-denied is canonically defensible because a denied deity of Christ collapses the gospel: a created savior cannot bear the wrath of an infinite God against sin, and worship of a creature would be idolatry per Exod 20:3-5. Public-record carriers of denial across history: Arianism in the fourth century (Christ as the highest created being); Jehovah's Witnesses (the Watchtower's New World Translation renders John 1:1 'the Word was a god', and the JW catechism teaches that Jesus Christ is Michael the archangel, a created spirit being); Christadelphians (founded by John Thomas; Christ as exalted man with no pre-existence); Unitarian Universalists (Christ as moral teacher only); classical Socinianism in the sixteenth century (Faustus Socinus); Iglesia ni Cristo (Felix Manalo's Filipino sect that denies Christ's deity and teaches Manalo as the angel of Revelation 7); Latter-day Saints Mormonism (a polytheistic distortion in which Christ is one god among many, the spirit-brother of Lucifer); Christian Science (Mary Baker Eddy treats Christ as the divine idea, not God incarnate). All of these are treated as outside historic Christianity by every tracked lineage. Fruit-vs-precision nuance: most believers cannot articulate homoousios, but they pray to Jesus, worship Jesus, and confess him as Lord (Rom 10:9), which is functional confession of his deity. The cult-marker line falls sharply on this question: denial of Christ's full deity is denial of the gospel itself.

stem_audit.notes: The statement 'Jesus Christ is fully and uniquely God, eternally begotten and not created, equal in essence with the Father' uses creedal phrasing ('eternally begotten and not created', 'equal in essence') drawn from the Nicene Creed. However, every clause is a direct lexical gloss of canonical text. 'Fully and uniquely God' restates John 1:1 and Col 2:9; 'eternally begotten' restates monogenēs G3439 in John 1:14, 18; 'not created' restates John 1:3 (panta di' autou egeneto, all things came into being through him, putting him on the Creator side of the divide); 'equal in essence' restates Phil 2:6 (isa theō, equal with God). The statement asserts the verdict, but every divine-attribute and Christological proposition does, and the lexical evidence supports the assertion.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: Jesus Christ is fully and truly God. He is not a creature, not a lesser god, not an exalted angel, and not merely a great teacher. He is God the Son, sharing the same divine nature as God the Father. The strongest reason from Scripture is the way the Bible directly calls Jesus God. John 1:1 opens, 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God,' and John 1:14 says, 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.' The Word is Jesus, and the Bible says the Word was God. In John 20:28, when Thomas sees the risen Jesus, he says, 'My Lord and my God.' Jesus accepts the confession instead of correcting it. In Colossians 2:9, Paul writes that in Christ 'the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily,' meaning the complete divine nature lives in Jesus in his physical body. In Hebrews 1:8, God the Father addresses the Son and says, 'Your throne, O God, is forever and ever.' Titus 2:13 calls Jesus 'our great God and Savior.' The strongest counterargument inside the Bible is the set of passages where Jesus seems lower than the Father. John 14:28 says, 'The Father is greater than I.' Mark 13:32 says that not even the Son knows the day or the hour, but only the Father. Critics ask, if Jesus is fully God, how can he not know the hour. Philippians 2:6 to 8 supplies the answer: Jesus, while remaining truly God, took on a true human nature and humbled himself, taking the form of a servant. As truly human, he experienced limitations the way humans do. As truly God, he never stopped being God. John 14:28 is about Jesus's role as the sent Son submitting to the Father during his earthly mission, not about his nature being less divine. The Bible holds both truths together. John 1:1 says he was God. Philippians 2:7 says he made himself nothing. Both are true, and the resurrection vindicates the full deity that was always there.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every major historic Christian lineage confesses that Jesus Christ is fully God, of the same divine essence as the Father. This is the original Trinitarian boundary that was fixed at the Council of Nicaea in AD 325 against Arius, who taught that the Son was the highest created being. The Nicene Creed declares Jesus 'true God from true God, begotten not made, of one essence with the Father.' The Athanasian Creed, which followed, expands this with even stronger language about the co-equal deity of the Son. Patristic authors who defended this doctrine include Athanasius of Alexandria, the Cappadocian Fathers (Basil, Gregory of Nazianzus, Gregory of Nyssa), Hilary of Poitiers, and Augustine. The Catholic Catechism teaches that Jesus is 'consubstantial with the Father,' true God and true man. The Lutheran Book of Concord, Augsburg Confession Article III, confesses the Son as 'true God and true man.' The Anglican 39 Articles, Article II, calls the Son 'the Word of the Father, of one substance with the Father.' The Reformed confessions (Westminster, Belgic, Heidelberg) all affirm the same. Methodists in their Articles of Religion repeat the Anglican formula. Anabaptists at Dordrecht confess the eternal Son. Pentecostals in the Assemblies of God statement teach the deity of Christ. Eastern Orthodoxy holds the Nicene Creed as the central confession. There is no historic Christian tradition that denies this. Public-record carriers of denial are uniformly treated as outside historic Christianity. Arianism was condemned at Nicaea in AD 325 and again at Constantinople in AD 381. Modern carriers include Jehovah's Witnesses, whose New World Translation renders John 1:1 as 'the Word was a god,' and whose teaching that Jesus is Michael the archangel makes Jesus a created spirit being. Christadelphians teach Jesus as an exalted human with no pre-existence. Unitarian Universalists reduce Jesus to a moral teacher. Mormonism teaches a polytheistic system in which Jesus is one god among many, the spirit-brother of Lucifer, born of literal sexual union between God the Father and Mary. Iglesia ni Cristo, the Filipino sect founded by Felix Manalo, denies the deity of Christ outright. Christian Science, founded by Mary Baker Eddy, treats Christ as a divine idea rather than as God incarnate. Every tracked Christian lineage classifies these groups as denying the gospel itself, not merely as in-house disputes.

scripture.key_term: καὶ θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος; theos G2316; logos G3056; eimi G1510 imperfect indicative active 3s
scripture.force: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' Three uses of the imperfect ēn for the Word, signalling continuous past existence, distinguished from aoristic ginomai ('came to be') used of creatures at 1:3 and 1:14. The third clause has theos as predicate of ho logos; the word order with theos fronted is emphatic, and the chiastic structure with v.1b prevents reading it as 'a god'. Logos is identified with God in essence while distinguished from God the Father as a personal counterpart (pros ton theon)

scripture.key_term: πάντα δι' αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο καὶ χωρὶς αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο οὐδὲ ἕν; pas G3956; ginomai G1096 aorist middle indicative 3s; chōris G5565
scripture.force: 'All things came into being through him, and apart from him not one thing came into being.' Aorist ginomai of all created reality contrasted with the imperfect ēn of the Word in v.1. Universal scope plus the categorical negation (oude hen, 'not even one thing') places the Word on the Creator side of the Creator-creature divide. If he made everything, he is not part of the everything

scripture.key_term: ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο; sarx G4561; ginomai G1096 aorist middle indicative 3s; monogenēs G3439
scripture.force: 'And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father.' The same Word who was God in 1:1 became (ginomai, aorist) human flesh. Doxa ('glory') is the same word used for the Shekinah glory of YHWH in the LXX. Monogenēs identifies him as the unique Son in a way no creature is

scripture.key_term: μονογενὴς θεὸς ὁ ὢν εἰς τὸν κόλπον τοῦ πατρός; monogenēs G3439; theos G2316
scripture.force: 'No one has ever seen God; the only God, who is at the Father's side, has made him known.' The earliest and best manuscripts (P66, P75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) read monogenēs theos ('only-begotten God'), with a minority later reading 'only Son'. The reading 'only God' is the harder reading and is widely accepted by modern critical editions. Calls Jesus theos directly while distinguishing him from the Father at whose side he is

scripture.key_term: πρὶν Ἀβραὰμ γενέσθαι ἐγὼ εἰμί; egō eimi G1473 plus G1510 present indicative active 1s; ginomai G1096 aorist middle infinitive
scripture.force: 'Before Abraham was, I am.' Jesus contrasts the aorist ginomai ('came to be', creaturely mode) of Abraham with the present absolute eimi ('I am', divine self-existence). Direct echo of Exod 3:14 LXX ego eimi ho on. The audience's immediate response in v.59 (picking up stones to kill him for blasphemy) shows they understood the claim as appropriation of the divine name

scripture.key_term: ὁ κύριός μου καὶ ὁ θεός μου; kyrios G2962; theos G2316
scripture.force: Thomas addresses the risen Jesus directly: 'My Lord and my God.' Both nominatives with article function as vocative (a standard Koine pattern). Jesus does not correct the confession but commends it in v.29 ('blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed'). If Jesus were not God, accepting this address would be blasphemy; he accepts it. Climactic Christological confession of John's gospel

scripture.key_term: ἐν αὐτῷ κατοικεῖ πᾶν τὸ πλήρωμα τῆς θεότητος σωματικῶς; plērōma G4138; theotēs G2320; sōmatikōs G4985; katoikeō G2730 present active indicative 3s
scripture.force: 'For in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily.' Theotēs G2320 (used only here in the NT) is the abstract noun for the divine nature itself, distinct from theiotēs G2305 ('divine quality', Rom 1:20). Plērōma ('fullness') modified by pan ('all') leaves nothing of the divine nature outside Christ. Sōmatikōs ('bodily') locates this divine fullness in the incarnate body of Jesus. The present tense katoikei marks ongoing dwelling, not a temporary visitation

scripture.key_term: εἰκὼν τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ἀοράτου; eikōn G1504; aoratos G517; prōtotokos G4416
scripture.force: 'He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation.' Eikōn is not a mere copy but the visible representation that shares the nature of what it images. Prōtotokos in the LXX (Ps 89:27) signifies preeminence and rank, not chronological birth-order; v.16 confirms this by saying all things were created in, through, and for him. Christ is on the Creator side, not the created side

scripture.key_term: ἐν αὐτῷ ἐκτίσθη τὰ πάντα; ktizō G2936 aorist passive indicative 3s; pas G3956
scripture.force: 'For in him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible.' Aorist passive of ktizō with pas. Every created thing was created in, through (di' autou), and for (eis auton) him. If everything was made through him, he is not part of everything that was made. Parallels John 1:3

scripture.key_term: πρὸς δὲ τὸν υἱόν Ὁ θρόνος σου ὁ θεὸς εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα; ho theos as vocative; thronos G2362
scripture.force: 'But of the Son he says, Your throne, O God, is forever and ever.' Quoting Ps 45:6 LXX. The Father addresses the Son as ho theos. Whether ho theos is read as vocative ('O God') or as nominative ('God is your throne'), both readings on the table identify the Son with God; the vocative is the more natural Greek reading and is followed by every major translation. The point of the chain of OT quotations in Heb 1:5-13 is to demonstrate the Son's superiority to angels, which would collapse if the Son were himself an angel or creature

scripture.key_term: ἀπαύγασμα τῆς δόξης καὶ χαρακτὴρ τῆς ὑποστάσεως αὐτοῦ; apaugasma G541; charaktēr G5481; hypostasis G5287
scripture.force: 'He is the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature.' Apaugasma (the outshining of light from its source, sharing its substance) plus charaktēr (the exact impressed image, as a coin from a die) plus hypostasis (the underlying substance). The Son shares the very substance of the Father, not merely resembles him

scripture.key_term: ἐν μορφῇ θεοῦ ὑπάρχων οὐχ ἁρπαγμὸν ἡγήσατο τὸ εἶναι ἴσα θεῷ; morphē G3444; harpagmos G725; isos G2470
scripture.force: 'Who, though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped.' Morphē (Greek philosophical term for essential form, not external shape) plus hyparchōn (present active participle of continuous prior existence) places Christ in the divine essence before the incarnation. To einai isa theō ('the being equal with God') is what he already possessed, not something to be seized

scripture.key_term: ἑαυτὸν ἐκένωσεν μορφὴν δούλου λαβών; kenoō G2758 aorist active indicative 3s; doulos G1401
scripture.force: 'But emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men.' The kenosis is voluntary self-humbling, not loss of divine nature. He took (labōn) the morphē of a servant while remaining what he was; he did not exchange one form for another. The same morphē-language unites v.6 (form of God) and v.7 (form of a servant), framing the incarnation as addition of humanity, not subtraction of deity

scripture.key_term: τοῦ μεγάλου θεοῦ καὶ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ; megas G3173; theos G2316; sōtēr G4990
scripture.force: 'Waiting for our blessed hope, the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ.' Single article tou governs both theos and sōtēr joined by kai, both genitives referring to one person, identified by name as Iēsou Christou. The construction (one article, two singular non-proper nouns of the same case joined by kai) regularly identifies both nouns with the same person. Jesus Christ is called both 'our great God' and 'Savior'

scripture.key_term: τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν καὶ σωτῆρος Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ; theos G2316; sōtēr G4990
scripture.force: 'By the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ.' Same construction as Titus 2:13: one article tou governing theos and sōtēr joined by kai, both genitives identifying one person, Jesus Christ. Peter calls Jesus 'our God and Savior'. Doubled by the parallel construction tou kyriou hēmōn kai sōtēros Iēsou Christou at 2 Pet 1:11 and 3:18

scripture.key_term: ὁ ὢν ἐπὶ πάντων θεὸς εὐλογητός εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας; ho ōn; theos G2316; eulogētos G2128
scripture.force: 'To them belong the patriarchs, and from their race, according to the flesh, is the Christ, who is God over all, blessed forever.' The participial phrase ho ōn ('who is') most naturally takes the immediately preceding ho Christos as antecedent. Punctuation that severs ho ōn from Christos to make it an independent doxology to the Father is grammatically strained: ho ōn elsewhere in Paul (2 Cor 11:31) clearly modifies the foregoing subject. The natural reading calls Christ ho epi pantōn theos, 'God over all'

scripture.key_term: ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ ἄλφα καὶ τὸ ὦ; alpha; ōmega; pantokratōr G3841
scripture.force: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega, says the Lord God, who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty.' Spoken by God. The same self-designation 'the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last' is then applied to Jesus at Rev 22:13 in his own first-person speech, with v.16 identifying the speaker as Jesus. The same divine identifier covers both the Father and the Son

scripture.key_term: ἐγὼ τὸ ἄλφα καὶ τὸ ὦ ὁ πρῶτος καὶ ὁ ἔσχατος ἡ ἀρχὴ καὶ τὸ τέλος; prōtos G4413; eschatos G2078
scripture.force: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega, the first and the last, the beginning and the end.' Speaker identified at v.16 as Jesus ('I, Jesus, have sent my angel to testify to you'). The titles ho prōtos kai ho eschatos directly echo Isa 44:6 LXX where YHWH says 'I am the first and the last; besides me there is no god'. Jesus appropriates the divine self-naming

scripture.key_term: περὶ δὲ τῆς ἡμέρας ἐκείνης ἢ τῆς ὥρας οὐδεὶς οἶδεν οὐδὲ ὁ υἱός; oida G1492 perfect active indicative 3s
scripture.force: 'But concerning that day or that hour, no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.' Read as a denial of omniscience, it would compromise Christ's full deity. Read with Phil 2:7 and the doctrine of the two natures (Christ truly God and truly human), the Son's not-knowing is predicated of his human nature during the earthly mission, while his divine omniscience is preserved. Complicating passage requiring the kenosis framework to integrate

scripture.key_term: ὁ πατὴρ μείζων μού ἐστιν; megas G3173 comparative meizōn
scripture.force: 'The Father is greater than I.' Read as a denial of equality of essence, it would compromise full deity. Read with John 10:30 ('I and the Father are one'), 14:9 ('whoever has seen me has seen the Father'), and Phil 2:6, the comparison is functional during the earthly mission (the sent Son submitting to the Sender) rather than ontological. The same gospel that has John 14:28 also has John 1:1 and 20:28; the canonical context fixes the reading

scripture.key_term: ὅταν δὲ ὑποταγῇ αὐτῷ τὰ πάντα τότε καὶ αὐτὸς ὁ υἱὸς ὑποταγήσεται; hypotassō G5293 aorist passive subjunctive 3s
scripture.force: 'When all things are subjected to him, then the Son himself will also be subjected to him who put all things in subjection under him, that God may be all in all.' Read as ontological subordination, would compromise full deity. Read with Phil 2:6-11 and the role-vs-essence distinction, the subjection is functional within the redemptive economy: the Son as the messianic king delivering the kingdom to the Father. Complicates only on a flat reading; coheres on the canonical reading

scripture.key_term: ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἕν ἐσμεν; hen G1520 neuter; eimi G1510 present indicative active 1p
scripture.force: 'I and the Father are one.' Hen is neuter ('one thing'), not masculine ('one person'), so it denotes unity of essence and action rather than identity of person. The audience's response in v.31 (taking up stones to stone him for blasphemy because 'you, being a man, make yourself God', v.33) confirms they understood the claim as a deity claim. Jesus does not retreat from it

scripture.key_term: ἴσον ἑαυτὸν ποιῶν τῷ θεῷ; isos G2470; poieō G4160 present active participle
scripture.force: 'This was why the Jews were seeking all the more to kill him, because not only was he breaking the Sabbath, but he was even calling God his own Father, making himself equal with God.' The narrative voice of John reports that Jesus was making himself isos ('equal') with God. The Greek term is the same as in Phil 2:6. Jesus does not correct the inference in vv.19-30; he develops it, claiming to do what the Father does (v.19) and to receive the honor due the Father (v.23)

scripture.key_term: וְקָרָא שְׁמוֹ פֶּלֶא יוֹעֵץ אֵל גִּבּוֹר אֲבִי־עַד שַׂר־שָׁלוֹם; el H410; gibbor H1368; ab H1; ad H5703
scripture.force: 'For to us a child is born, to us a son is given... and his name shall be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace.' El gibbor is used elsewhere only of YHWH (Isa 10:21). Applied to the messianic child. Old Testament canonical anchor for the deity of the coming Messiah

scripture.key_term: ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι Ἰησοῦ πᾶν γόνυ κάμψῃ; gony G1119; kamptō G2578 aorist active subjunctive 3s; pas G3956
scripture.force: 'So that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow.' Quoting Isa 45:23, where YHWH says 'to me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall confess'. Paul applies this YHWH-text directly to Jesus. The exclusive worship Isaiah claims for YHWH is given to Jesus without contradiction because Jesus is YHWH the Son

competing_lens_verdicts.notes: The Nicene-Athanasian tradition (Athanasius, the Cappadocians, Hilary, Augustine, Aquinas, Calvin, the Reformers) reads John 1:1, 20:28, Col 2:9, Heb 1:8, Titus 2:13, 2 Pet 1:1, Phil 2:6 as direct lexical predications of full deity to Christ. The complicating texts (Mark 13:32, John 14:28, 1 Cor 15:28) are handled by the doctrine of the two natures and the role-vs-essence distinction. This is the universal historic Christian reading

Arius (4th century) and his modern heirs (Jehovah's Witnesses, Christadelphians, Iglesia ni Cristo, classical Socinianism) take Mark 13:32, John 14:28, 1 Cor 15:28, Col 1:15 (prōtotokos), and the messianic-subordination texts as ontological and recast theos in John 1:1 and elsewhere as a lesser divine title or a creaturely status. The JW New World Translation renders John 1:1c 'the Word was a god'. This reading collapses on the canonical evidence: it cannot integrate John 1:3 (all things made through him), John 20:28 (Thomas's confession accepted), Col 2:9 (pan to plērōma tēs theotētos), Heb 1:8 (the Father addresses the Son as ho theos), Titus 2:13 and 2 Pet 1:1 (the single-article construction), and Phil 2:10 quoting Isa 45:23 of Jesus. Condemned at Nicaea AD 325 and Constantinople AD 381

Modalism (Sabellius, Praxeas, modern Oneness Pentecostals) affirms the full deity of Christ but denies the distinction of persons, holding that the Father, Son, and Spirit are three modes of the one God. On the bare proposition that Christ is fully God, modalists agree with classical Trinitarianism; the disagreement is over the distinct personhood of the Son, which is a different doctrine. Tertullian's Adversus Praxean targets this view

Kenotic Christology (19th-century Thomasius, modern Stephen Davis, Ronald Feenstra) holds that at the incarnation the Son voluntarily set aside certain divine attributes (omniscience, omnipresence) while retaining others, thus affirming full deity in essence while explaining the limitations evident in Mark 13:32 and John 14:28. Historically contested by classical Trinitarians who use the two-natures framework instead. Either framework lands at full deity affirmed; only Arianism denies

hermeneutics.notes: The verdict rests on a pan-canonical lemma network. Theos G2316 is directly predicated of Jesus at John 1:1, John 1:18, John 20:28, Heb 1:8, Titus 2:13, 2 Pet 1:1, and (most natural reading) Rom 9:5. Theotēs G2320 (the abstract noun for the divine nature) is predicated of Christ in its fullness at Col 2:9. Divine titles and prerogatives are applied to Christ: I AM (egō eimi, John 8:58, echoing Exod 3:14), Alpha and Omega / first and last (Rev 22:13, echoing Isa 44:6), receiving universal worship (Phil 2:10 quoting Isa 45:23), being the agent of creation (John 1:3, Col 1:16, Heb 1:2), holding all things together (Col 1:17). Analogia scripturae is invoked at the complicating texts (Mark 13:32, John 14:28, 1 Cor 15:28, Col 1:15 prōtotokos), where the two-natures framework and the role-vs-essence distinction integrate them without denying full deity. Progressive revelation operates: Isa 9:6 and Isa 7:14 (Immanuel = God with us) anticipate the deity of the Messiah, the gospels narrate it, the epistles theologize it, Revelation seals it. No non-default hermeneutic is required; grammatico-historical method on the canon itself is sufficient

counter_witness.anchor: Nicene Creed AD 325 (CCEL NPNF Series 2 Vol 14); Athanasius, Four Discourses Against the Arians (NPNF2-04); Augustine, De Trinitate; Tertullian, Adversus Praxean; Hilary, De Trinitate
counter_witness.key_phrase: Nicene Creed: 'true God from true God, begotten not made, of one essence with the Father, through whom all things were made'. Athanasius defends co-eternal and co-essential deity of the Son against Arius

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church paragraphs 422 to 483 on the Son of God become man; CCC 464 to 469 on the Nicene Creed (vatican.va)
counter_witness.key_phrase: CCC 464: 'The unique and altogether singular event of the Incarnation of the Son of God does not mean that Jesus Christ is part God and part man.' CCC 465: he is true God and true man, consubstantial with the Father

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession Article III, Of the Son of God (bookofconcord.org); Formula of Concord Solid Declaration VIII
counter_witness.key_phrase: Augsburg III: 'The Son of God, did assume the human nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin Mary, so that there are two natures, the divine and the human, inseparably enjoined in one Person, one Christ, true God and true man'

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article II, Of the Word or Son of God, which was made very Man (churchofengland.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Article II: 'The Son, which is the Word of the Father, begotten from everlasting of the Father, the very and eternal God, and of one substance with the Father, took Man's nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin'

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith 8.2; Belgic Confession Article 10; Heidelberg Catechism Q&A 33-35
counter_witness.key_phrase: WCF 8.2: 'The Son of God, the second person in the Trinity, being very and eternal God, of one substance and equal with the Father, did, when the fullness of time was come, take upon him man's nature'

counter_witness.anchor: United Methodist Articles of Religion, Article II, Of the Word, or Son of God, Who Was Made Very Man (umc.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: 'The Son, who is the Word of the Father, the very and eternal God, of one substance with the Father, took man's nature in the womb of the blessed Virgin.' Wesley's abridgement preserves the substance language verbatim

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession 1632 Article 3, Of the Restoration of Man through the Promise of the Coming of Christ; Schleitheim is silent on Christology
counter_witness.key_phrase: Dordrecht confesses the eternal Son sent by the Father, born of Mary, the true Messiah and Savior. The Anabaptist mainstream affirmed Nicene Christology even where the Schleitheim minimalism omits explicit Trinitarian language

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths, Truth 2, The One True God, and Truth 3, The Deity of the Lord Jesus Christ (ag.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: AG Truth 2: the Godhead exists eternally in three persons. Truth 3: 'The Lord Jesus Christ is the eternal Son of God.' Specifically affirms his deity, virgin birth, sinless life, miraculous ministry, substitutionary atonement, resurrection, exaltation

counter_witness.anchor: OCA, The Orthodox Faith, Doctrine and Scripture, on Jesus Christ (oca.org); Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed as recited in Orthodox liturgy
counter_witness.key_phrase: Orthodoxy holds the Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed at the center of its liturgy: Jesus Christ is true God of true God, begotten not made, consubstantial with the Father. 'I and the Father are one' (Jn 10:30) is read with full Trinitarian weight

web.quote: John 1:1 'kai theos en ho logos'. theos G2316 as predicate of ho logos G3056. Word-order with theos fronted is emphatic. Imperfect en signals continuous prior existence of the Word as God
John 20:28 'ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou'. Both articular nominatives functioning as vocative. Thomas addresses risen Jesus as Lord and God; Jesus commends the confession at v.29
Col 2:9 'pan to plērōma tēs theotētos sōmatikōs'. theotēs G2320 = abstract noun for the divine nature itself, distinct from theiotēs G2305. Whole fullness of deity dwells bodily in Christ
Heb 1:8 'pros de ton huion ho thronos sou ho theos'. The Father addresses the Son as ho theos. Quoting Ps 45:6 LXX. The Son is identified with God in the very framework that contrasts him with angels
John 1:18 monogenēs theos. Earliest manuscripts (P66, P75, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) read 'only-begotten God'. Calls Jesus theos directly while distinguishing him from the Father at whose side he is
Titus 2:13 'tou megalou theou kai sōtēros hēmōn Iēsou Christou'. Single article tou governs two nouns joined by kai, both genitives referring to one person, Jesus Christ as our great God and Savior
2 Pet 1:1 'tou theou hēmōn kai sōtēros Iēsou Christou'. Same single-article-two-nouns construction as Titus 2:13. Peter calls Jesus 'our God and Savior'. Doubled at 2 Pet 1:11, 3:18

Phil 2:6 'en morphē theou hyparchōn'. morphē G3444 (essential form, not external shape) plus present participle hyparchōn (continuous prior existence). 'to einai isa theō' = being equal with God already possessed

Col 1:15 eikōn G1504 of the invisible God; prōtotokos G4416 of all creation. Eikōn shares the nature of what it images. Prōtotokos signifies preeminence (cf. Ps 89:27 LXX), confirmed by v.16: all things made in him

Rom 9:5 'ho ōn epi pantōn theos eulogētos'. Natural antecedent of ho ōn is the immediately preceding ho Christos. Calls Christ 'God over all, blessed forever'. Punctuation severing this is grammatically strained
theotēs G2320, NT hapax at Col 2:9. The abstract noun for the divine nature itself, distinguished from theiotēs G2305 ('divine quality') by carrying the full essence-content
monogenēs G3439, 'unique, one-of-a-kind'. Applied to the Son of God five times in John (1:14, 1:18, 3:16, 3:18, 1 Jn 4:9). Marks Jesus as the Son in a way no creature is

CCC on the Son of God: Jesus is true God and true man, consubstantial with the Father. The Nicene Creed remains the central confession of the Catholic faith. Verified against the CCC table of contents
AG Statement of Fundamental Truths 3: 'The Lord Jesus Christ is the eternal Son of God.' Affirms deity, virgin birth, sinless life, miraculous ministry, substitutionary atonement, bodily resurrection
OCA on Jesus Christ: 'I and the Father are one' (Jn 10.30) read with full Nicene weight. Orthodoxy confesses Jesus as true God of true God, consubstantial with the Father, in the Symbol of Faith
